slides.title: 10,000 Strangers, One Problem
slides.subtitle: How the Internet turns huge crowds of ordinary people into problem-solvers — and why scale, not expertise, is the superpower
slides.footerLeft: AP Computer Science Principles · College Board CED (Effective Fall 2023)
slides.script: Topic 5.4, Crowdsourcing — one day, one learning objective. Big Idea 5 has so far covered effects, the digital divide, and bias; today is the optimistic chapter: how the Internet lets enormous crowds of ordinary people attack problems no individual or lab could touch. The through-line is scale — LO IOC-1.E asks students to explain how people participate in problem-solving processes at scale, and every slide keeps that word in view. By the end of the period, students can define crowdsourcing and citizen science and classify any crowd project the exam describes.

slides.heading: By the End of Today You Will...

items.text: Explain how widespread access to information and public data helps people identify problems, develop solutions, and share results (LO IOC-1.E)
Define crowdsourcing and citizen science, and explain how collaboration via computing enhances what people can accomplish (LO IOC-1.E)

slides.script: Two targets, both inside IOC-1.E. First, the fuel: open information and public data make it easier to find problems, build solutions, and spread what was learned. Second, the machinery: the two named ideas — crowdsourcing and citizen science — plus the principle underneath both, that collaboration via computing enhances human capabilities. Exam items on this topic are almost always classification: read a scenario, decide whether it is crowdsourcing, and name what the crowd is contributing.

slides.prompt: 10,000 strangers on the Internet will each give you exactly 60 seconds of their time — one minute each, then they are gone forever. Design a project that becomes POSSIBLE only at that scale.
slides.subprompt: Sketch it: what does each stranger DO in their minute, and why would 10 helpers — or even 100 — not be enough? Name the thing only a crowd can give you.
slides.directions: Write your project idea and the 60-second task. Be ready to defend why the scale is essential.
slides.script: A few minutes, on your own — the constraint is the point: each helper gives one minute, so the task must be tiny, and there are ten thousand of them, so the tiny tasks must add up to something huge. Students typically invent photographing every storm drain in the city, reporting gas prices, tagging photos, or pledging a dollar each — every one of those is a real crowdsourcing model they will meet by name today. Push on the second question: if the project still works with 100 people, it was never a crowd project. Keep the designs visible; the whole lesson is the College Board's vocabulary for what students just invented — obtaining input from a large number of people via the Internet.

slides.heading: What You'll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Guided Notes have 3 sections that match today's lesson. Green = CB required.

sections.title: Problem-Solving at Scale
sections.description: Open information and public data, and how collaboration via computing enhances human capability (EK IOC-1.E.1, E.5).

sections.title: Crowdsourcing and Citizen Science
sections.description: The definition, the two famous crowd models — volunteer data and new funding-style collaborations — and how to classify projects (EK IOC-1.E.2–E.4, E.6).

sections.title: Deep Dive: From Bird Counts to Published Science
sections.description: How millions of amateur observations survive quality control and end up in peer-reviewed research.

slides.note: Fill in your Guided Notes as we go. Absent or lost your packet? A digital copy lives on the Topic 5.4 Guided Notes page at apcsexamprep.com.
slides.script: Today's Guided Notes map: three sections. Two are green and CB-required — problem-solving at scale, then the crowdsourcing and citizen-science models with a classification workout. The third is the blue deep dive tracing volunteer bird counts all the way into published journals — not required by the College Board, but it makes 'citizen science' feel real. Packets stay open all period; a digital copy lives on the Topic 5.4 Guided Notes page at apcsexamprep.com for anyone absent.

slides.label: Problem-Solving at Scale
slides.kicker: Open Data, Open Problems · Learning Objective IOC-1.E
slides.script: Section one. Before naming crowdsourcing, students need the two enabling ideas: widespread access to information and public data, and collaboration via computing. Together they explain why the bell-ringer projects only work at Internet scale — the data is open to everyone, and the network coordinates thousands of contributors at once.

slides.heading: Key Vocabulary (LO IOC-1.E)

terms.definition: The practice of obtaining input or information from a large number of people via the Internet (EK IOC-1.E.4).
Research conducted in whole or part by distributed individuals — many not scientists — who contribute relevant data using their own computing devices (EK IOC-1.E.3).

slides.script: Two terms carry the whole topic, and both definitions are pure College Board language. Crowdsourcing has two non-negotiable ingredients: a LARGE NUMBER of people, and the Internet as the channel — students should underline both, since exam distractors attack each one. Citizen science is a specific flavor of crowdsourcing where the crowd contributes DATA to real research: the contributors are distributed, many are not scientists, and they use their own computing devices. Notice what is missing from both definitions — expertise. That absence is deliberate, and it is tested.

slides.heading: Why the Crowd Can Do What You Can't
bullets: Widespread access to information and public data facilitates the identification of problems, the development of solutions, and the dissemination of results (EK IOC-1.E.1).
Human capabilities can be enhanced by collaboration via computing — the network doesn't just connect people, it multiplies what they can accomplish together (EK IOC-1.E.5).
The collaboration skills from Topic 1.1 — communication, consensus building, conflict resolution (LO CRD-1.C) — still apply; computing lets them run among millions of strangers.
Your bell-ringer project worked the same way: one tiny task, repeated by thousands of connected people, produced something no single person ever could.
slides.script: Two EKs and one callback on this slide. First, EK IOC-1.E.1's chain: when information and public data are open to everyone, more eyes find the problems, more hands build the solutions, and the results travel farther — a city posting its own traffic data is inviting the public to out-engineer city hall. Second, EK IOC-1.E.5: collaboration via computing enhances human capabilities — the same interpersonal skills students practiced in Topic 1.1 under LO CRD-1.C, except the team is now the whole Internet. The bell ringer is the proof of concept: none of the 10,000 strangers did anything hard, and the crowd still did something impossible.

capture.q: Open access to public data makes it easier to find problems, build solutions, and ______.
capture.a: spread what was learned to everyone who needs it
what humans are able to accomplish together

capture.q: Computing changes Topic 1.1's collaboration skills by letting them operate ______.
capture.a: across enormous online crowds, not just small teams

slides.heading: “Crowdsourcing Means Asking Experts Online”
slides.misconception: Crowdsourcing works by locating the smartest specialists on the Internet — a crowdsourced project is really just a clever way to find a few brilliant experts.
slides.whyItHappens: In daily life, help means finding the most qualified person — a doctor, a mechanic. It feels natural to assume online help works the same way, just with better search.
slides.correction: The CED defines crowdsourcing by SIZE, not credentials: obtaining input or information from a LARGE NUMBER of people via the Internet (EK IOC-1.E.4). Citizen science says it outright — many contributors may not be scientists (EK IOC-1.E.3). The power is the crowd itself.
slides.example: A bird-count app doesn't need ornithologists — it needs a million ordinary people, each photographing whatever lands in their own backyard. No expert team could ever stand in a million backyards at once.
slides.script: This is the trap the exam sets most often for this topic, so it gets its own slide. The misconception survives because it matches how help works offline — you find the one most qualified person. Crowdsourcing inverts that: the definition in EK IOC-1.E.4 hangs on a large number of people and the Internet, and says nothing about qualifications, while EK IOC-1.E.3 explicitly notes that citizen-science contributors may not be scientists. The mechanism is coverage and volume, not credentials: a million backyards beats one laboratory. Students should explain in their packet why the expert version of this claim fails the definition.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — The Power of Scale
slides.subheading: These map to LO IOC-1.E. Complete on your Guided Notes.
prompts: Your bell-ringer project needed 10,000 strangers rather than 10 experts. Using the definition of crowdsourcing, explain why the strangers' lack of expertise doesn't disqualify the project.

A city posts its bus-delay records as free public data. Within a month, riders spot the worst routes, volunteer programmers release a rerouting app, and a local news site publishes the findings. Name the three things widespread data access facilitated here.
In one sentence: what can collaboration via computing do that a room of 30 talented people cannot?
slides.directions: Answer in complete sentences. Then check yourself with the matching CFUs on the Topic 5.4 page.
slides.script: Five minutes, full sentences on the Guided Notes. One: the definition asks for a large number of people via the Internet — expertise appears nowhere in it, so the strangers qualify perfectly. Two: this is EK IOC-1.E.1's chain in the wild — identification of problems (the worst routes), development of solutions (the rerouting app), dissemination of results (the news story). Three: any answer touching scale earns it — computing coordinates contributors beyond any single room, at distances and volumes no in-person team can match. A few students will be called on to share before the next section.

slides.label: Two Shapes of Crowd Power
slides.kicker: Data, Dollars, and the Crowd · Learning Objective IOC-1.E
slides.script: Section two — the classification skill the exam actually tests. Every crowd project fits one definition, but the CED highlights two famous shapes: citizen science, where the crowd contributes data to research, and the newer collaboration models like crowdfunding, where the crowd contributes money or ideas. The skill is reading a scenario and naming the shape.

slides.heading: One Definition, Two Famous Shapes
slides.subheading: Both fit the definition of crowdsourcing. What differs is what each contributor hands over (EK IOC-1.E.2–E.4, E.6).

left.title: Citizen science
left.definition: The crowd contributes DATA — observations gathered on their own computing devices
examples: Volunteers photograph birds, log rainfall, or report earthquake shaking
Many contributors are not scientists — and don't need to be
Science itself has been changed by distributed volunteers

right.title: Funding & input models
right.definition: The crowd contributes MONEY or ideas — new collaboration models the Internet created
examples: Thousands of small pledges launch a product or support a cause
Connecting businesses or social causes with funding
Also at scale: reviews, map edits, encyclopedia articles

slides.footer: AP TIP: Both are crowdsourcing — input or information from a large number of people via the Internet. Ask WHAT the crowd contributes to tell them apart.
slides.script: Here is the whole classification scheme. On the left, citizen science: distributed individuals, many of them non-scientists, contribute relevant data to real research using their own computing devices — and EK IOC-1.E.2 adds that science itself has been affected by this. On the right, the newer collaboration models from EK IOC-1.E.6: crowdfunding platforms that connect businesses or social causes with thousands of small funders, plus the broader input-at-scale family — reviews, map corrections, volunteer-written encyclopedias. Both sides satisfy the same EK IOC-1.E.4 definition; the sorting question is always what each contributor hands over. That one question cracks nearly every exam scenario on this topic.

capture.q: In citizen science, each volunteer's phone or laptop becomes ______.
capture.a: a tiny scientific instrument feeding one shared study

capture.q: Crowdfunding counts as crowdsourcing since the input collected is ______.
capture.a: many small pledges of money gathered online

capture.q: To classify any crowd project, ask what ______.
capture.a: each participant actually hands over to the project

slides.heading: Crowd or Not? Classify Five Projects
slides.context: Read each project. First ask: input from a large number of people via the Internet? If yes, name the model and what the crowd contributes.
rows: An app invites anyone to photograph birds; millions of sightings feed real research
Data — observations from their own devices
A site collects small pledges from 12,000 backers to launch a new board game
A navigation app merges live speed readings from millions of drivers' phones
A newspaper hires three meteorologists to write its daily forecast
A few hired experts — not a crowd
An online encyclopedia anyone can edit, checked by other volunteers
slides.script: Five projects, one question each: is this input from a large number of people via the Internet? The bird app is textbook citizen science — the crowd contributes observations from its own devices. The board game is EK IOC-1.E.6's funding model — the contribution is money, in thousands of small pieces. The navigation app is the sneaky one: drivers contribute data passively, but it is still a large number of people supplying information via the Internet. The newspaper fails the definition — three hired specialists are a team, not a crowd, no matter how skilled. The encyclopedia is input at scale: knowledge itself is the contribution. Students fill the blanked cells before answers are revealed; the reasoning matters more than the verdict.

slides.heading: Deep Dive: From Bird Counts to Published Science
bullets: Every year, hundreds of thousands of volunteers log bird sightings through an app — each record stamped with time, place, and photos from the volunteer's own device.
Uploads pass automated plausibility checks, and unusual reports are flagged for regional reviewers — quality control is what turns hobby photos into usable evidence.
Pooled across decades and continents, the records reveal patterns no laboratory could capture: whole species' winter ranges drifting north as the climate warms.
Hundreds of peer-reviewed papers have been built on volunteer bird data — distributed non-scientists are quietly co-authoring the scientific record (EK IOC-1.E.2).
slides.script: A deep dive to make EK IOC-1.E.2 concrete — this goes beyond what the exam requires. The pipeline is the interesting part: a volunteer's checklist is not trusted blindly; software checks whether the species is plausible for that date and place, and odd reports go to human reviewers before entering the dataset. That quality-control layer is the answer to the classic objection 'amateur data can't be science.' Once pooled, the dataset does what no funded expedition could — continuous coverage of a continent for decades — and real journals publish real findings from it. Fast finishers can sketch their own quality-control rule for the bell-ringer project.

capture.q: Volunteer observations become scientific evidence only after ______.
capture.a: screening and reviewer checks filter out bad records
long-term shifts invisible to any single observer

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Make the Classification
slides.subheading: These map to LO IOC-1.E. Complete on your Guided Notes.
prompts: A charity site gathers $5 donations from 40,000 people for storm relief; a research app gathers pollen photos from 40,000 allergy sufferers. Classify each project's model and name what its crowd contributes.
A friend dismisses the pollen project: 'That's not real science — none of those people have degrees.' Use the definition of citizen science to correct the friend.
Write the one question you should ask about ANY crowd-project scenario on the exam — and what the answer tells you.
slides.directions: Answer in complete sentences. Then check yourself with the matching CFUs on the Topic 5.4 page.
slides.script: Six minutes, full sentences. One: same crowd size, different contributions — the charity site is the funding model with money as the input, the pollen app is citizen science with data as the input; both are crowdsourcing. Two: the definition anticipates the friend's objection directly — citizen science is conducted by distributed individuals, many of whom may not be scientists, contributing relevant data on their own devices; degrees are not in the definition. Three: the one question is what the crowd contributes — or at the gate, whether a large number of people supply input via the Internet at all. Answers are reviewed as a class before the traps slide.

slides.heading: Common AP Traps
slides.subheading: Three ways Topic 5.4 loses points on the exam — one minute now, real points in May.

strategies.name: Experts not required
strategies.text: Crowdsourcing needs a LARGE NUMBER of people and the Internet — nothing else. Options requiring specialists or credentials contradict the definition and are distractors.

strategies.name: Data, not just opinions
strategies.text: Citizen-science volunteers contribute relevant DATA to research using their own computing devices — measurements and observations, not merely votes or feelings.

strategies.name: Scale is the mechanism
strategies.text: The tested idea is collaboration at scale: computing enhances human capability by coordinating huge crowds. A small hired team — however skilled — is not crowdsourcing.

slides.script: One minute, three traps. Trap one: distractors love to smuggle credentials into crowdsourcing — 'verified experts,' 'certified reviewers' — but the definition has exactly two ingredients, a large number of people and the Internet. Trap two: citizen science is a data pipeline, not a poll; if the crowd is only voting on favorites, it is crowdsourced input, but it is not contributing research data. Trap three: the exam rewards naming the mechanism — collaboration via computing at a scale no individual or small team can reach; three brilliant hires are collaboration, not crowdsourcing. Students write each trap in their own words in the packet.

slides.heading: Crowdsourcing, in One Slide
points: Widespread access to information and public data facilitates identifying problems, developing solutions, and disseminating results (EK IOC-1.E.1).
Citizen science: distributed individuals — many not scientists — contribute relevant data to research using their own computing devices (EK IOC-1.E.2–E.3).
Crowdsourcing is obtaining input or information from a large number of people via the Internet (EK IOC-1.E.4).
Human capabilities can be enhanced by collaboration via computing (EK IOC-1.E.5).
New collaboration models include connecting businesses or social causes with funding (EK IOC-1.E.6).
slides.next: Topic 5.5, Legal and Ethical Concerns: the crowd built it together — so who owns it, what may you reuse, and where is the legal line?
slides.script: The whole topic in one slide. Open information and public data invite everyone into the problem-solving process. Crowdsourcing is the umbrella — input or information from a large number of people via the Internet — and citizen science and crowdfunding are its two famous shapes, sorted by what the crowd contributes. Underneath everything sits EK IOC-1.E.5: collaboration via computing enhances human capabilities. Next class opens with the Topic 5.4 quiz, then Topic 5.5 asks who owns what the crowd creates. Before packing up, students run the exit check below — any unchecked box is tonight's review target on the Topic 5.4 page. Guided Notes are collected at the end of class.
ican: define crowdsourcing and spot its two required ingredients in any scenario (LO IOC-1.E)
explain how citizen science lets non-scientists contribute research data (LO IOC-1.E)
explain how collaboration via computing lets people solve problems at scale (LO IOC-1.E)

quiz.title: Topic 5.4 — Crowdsourcing — Quiz
quiz.instructions: Choose the single best answer. 6 questions, 10 minutes, no notes.

questions.stem: A transit app asks its two million riders to tap a button whenever their bus is late; the app merges the taps into a live delay map for the whole city. Which statement best explains why this is an example of crowdsourcing (EK IOC-1.E.4)?
options: The app verifies that each rider reporting a delay holds professional transit credentials
The app obtains input from a large number of people via the Internet, and the merged taps form information no single rider could produce
The app removes the need for human input by computing all delays from official schedule data
The app pays a small team of dispatchers to confirm every tap before it appears on the map
questions.rationale: EK IOC-1.E.4 defines crowdsourcing as obtaining input or information from a large number of people via the Internet — two million riders tapping through an app is exactly that, and the citywide delay map shows the scale payoff (EK IOC-1.E.5's collaboration via computing). A imports the expert misconception: the definition says nothing about credentials, and requiring them would defeat the model. C describes automation that eliminates the crowd — the opposite of crowdsourcing. D swaps the crowd for a small paid team; a handful of dispatchers is ordinary staffing, not a large number of people.

questions.stem: Four projects ask the public for help. Per EK IOC-1.E.3, which one is an example of citizen science?
options: A university lab emails its alumni asking them to share a fundraising page with friends
A polling firm hires 500 trained interviewers to conduct a door-to-door health survey
A magazine invites readers to vote online for their favorite nature photo of the year
Thousands of volunteers, most with no science background, upload water-quality readings from home test kits through a research app
questions.rationale: EK IOC-1.E.3 requires distributed individuals — many of whom may not be scientists — contributing relevant DATA to research using their own computing devices. D checks every box: thousands of distributed non-scientists, real water-quality data, submitted through their own devices. A collects publicity for fundraising, not research data. B uses hired, trained professionals doing in-person work — a paid workforce, not distributed volunteers on their own devices. C gathers opinions about photos; a popularity vote contributes no research data, which is the trap of treating any online participation as citizen science.

questions.stem: Which of the following best defines crowdsourcing (EK IOC-1.E.4)?
options: The practice of obtaining input or information from a large number of people via the Internet
The practice of dividing a program's tasks among several processors that run at the same time
The practice of hiring a small panel of certified experts to review a product before release
The practice of publishing a dataset so that only government scientists can analyze its contents
questions.rationale: A is the CED definition nearly verbatim: input or information, a large number of people, via the Internet. B defines parallel computing from Topic 4.3 — distributing work across processors, not across people. C fails both ingredients: a small panel is not a large number, and certification imports the expert misconception the definition deliberately omits. D inverts EK IOC-1.E.1 — restricting a dataset to government scientists is the opposite of the widespread access that lets the public identify problems and develop solutions.

questions.stem: An independent game studio cannot get a bank loan. It posts its project on a platform where 12,000 supporters each pledge a few dollars, fully funding the game in ten days. Per EK IOC-1.E.6, what does this scenario illustrate?
options: Citizen science, with each supporter contributing scientific data from a personal device
The digital divide, with unequal access to computing preventing banks from funding games
A new model of collaboration offered by crowdsourcing — connecting a business with funding
Machine learning, with the platform predicting which projects deserve automatic funding
questions.rationale: EK IOC-1.E.6 states that crowdsourcing offers new models for collaboration, such as connecting businesses or social causes with funding — 12,000 small pledges funding a studio that a bank turned down is that EK word for word. A names the wrong crowd shape: the supporters contributed money, not research data, so this is the funding model, not citizen science. B misapplies Topic 5.2 — nothing in the scenario involves unequal access to computing. D invents a mechanism the stem never describes; the platform connects people to a project, it does not predict or automate the funding decision.

questions.stem: A city publishes its pothole-repair and accident records as free public datasets. Within weeks, residents flag the most dangerous intersections, a developer releases a safer-routes app, and a neighborhood blog spreads the findings across the city. Per EK IOC-1.E.1, what does this sequence illustrate?
options: Only the identification of problems, since public data cannot legally be used in new software
Widespread access to public data facilitating identification of problems, development of solutions, and dissemination of results
Only the dissemination of results, since the residents and the developer never used the city's data
A security failure, since records a government collects should never be released to the public
questions.rationale: EK IOC-1.E.1 names three things widespread access to information and public data facilitates, and the stem stages all three in order: residents identify problems (dangerous intersections), a developer builds a solution (the app), and the blog disseminates the results. A invents a legal barrier that does not exist and ignores two-thirds of the sequence. C contradicts the stem — both the residents and the developer worked directly from the released datasets. D confuses open government data with a breach; publishing the city's own repair records is the deliberate access the EK describes, not a security failure.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements about problem-solving at scale are true? I. Human capabilities can be enhanced by collaboration via computing. II. Contributors to a citizen science project may use their own computing devices to submit data. III. Crowdsourcing requires every participant to be a subject-matter expert.
options: III only
II and III only
I, II, and III
I and II only
questions.rationale: Statement I is EK IOC-1.E.5 verbatim — collaboration via computing enhances human capabilities. Statement II comes straight from EK IOC-1.E.3 — citizen scientists contribute data using their own computing devices. Statement III is the expert misconception: EK IOC-1.E.4 defines crowdsourcing by a large number of people via the Internet, and EK IOC-1.E.3 notes many contributors may not be scientists, so expertise is optional — which eliminates A, B, and C. Students who feel the crowd 'must know what it's doing' pick an option containing III and lose the point.

guide.title: Topic 5.4 — Crowdsourcing — Teacher Guide
guide.subtitle: Big Idea 5: Impact of Computing · Topic 5.4 · 1 class period + assessment block
overview: Topic 5.4 is Big Idea 5's optimistic chapter: after effects, the digital divide, and bias, students see how the Internet lets enormous crowds of ordinary people participate in problem-solving at scale. The topic runs on one learning objective, IOC-1.E, and its six EKs supply a tight tool kit: open information and public data invite everyone into the process (E.1), crowdsourcing is input or information from a large number of people via the Internet (E.4), citizen science and funding-style models are its two famous shapes (E.2–E.3, E.6), and collaboration via computing enhances human capabilities (E.5).

The through-line is the bell ringer: given 10,000 strangers with 60 seconds each, students design a project that only works at that scale — inventing crowdsourcing's value proposition before it is named. Every later slide formalizes what they built: the vocabulary, the citizen-science versus funding-model split, a five-project classification table, and the exam's favorite trap (crowds do not need experts). The topic also closes the loop with Topic 1.1: the interpersonal collaboration skills of LO CRD-1.C, now operating at Internet scale.
los: LO IOC-1.E — Explain how people participate in problem-solving processes at scale.
eks: EK IOC-1.E.1 — Widespread access to information and public data facilitates the identification of problems, development of solutions, and dissemination of results.
EK IOC-1.E.2 — Science has been affected by using distributed and "citizen science" to solve scientific problems.

EK IOC-1.E.3 — Citizen science is scientific research conducted in whole or part by distributed individuals, many of whom may not be scientists, who contribute relevant data to research using their own computing devices.
EK IOC-1.E.4 — Crowdsourcing is the practice of obtaining input or information from a large number of people via the Internet.
EK IOC-1.E.5 — Human capabilities can be enhanced by collaboration via computing.
EK IOC-1.E.6 — Crowdsourcing offers new models for collaboration, such as connecting businesses or social causes with funding.
materials: Printed Topic 5.4 quiz (student copies) for the assessment block.
Optional: a teacher-device look at a public-data portal or a citizen-science app's live map for the deep dive.
prereqs: Topic 1.1 (Collaboration): students should already know the interpersonal skills of LO CRD-1.C — communication, consensus building, conflict resolution, negotiation. Topic 5.4 scales those same skills to Internet-sized crowds; the bell ringer assumes no prior crowdsourcing knowledge.

pacing.focus: Problem-solving at scale, then classifying crowdsourcing's two famous shapes (LO IOC-1.E)

segments.segment: Problem-solving at scale: vocab, open data, collaboration via computing, expert misconception (EK IOC-1.E.1, E.3–E.5)
Stop and Think on the power of scale
Two shapes of crowd power: citizen science vs. funding models + classify five projects (EK IOC-1.E.2–E.4, E.6)
Deep dive: from bird counts to published science (optional enrichment)
Stop and Think: make the classification

pacing.label: Flex / Assessment block — no new deck (uses the Topic Quiz)
pacing.focus: Not a numbered prep day. Run the Topic 5.4 quiz as the opener of the next class (doubles as spaced retrieval). The quiz ships as its own document, so no new deck or notes are needed.

misconceptions.heading: “Crowdsourcing means finding experts online”
misconceptions.myth: A crowdsourced project succeeds by locating the most qualified specialists on the Internet — the crowd is just a search tool for finding a few brilliant people.
misconceptions.reality: EK IOC-1.E.4 defines crowdsourcing by scale and channel — a large number of people, via the Internet — and never mentions credentials. EK IOC-1.E.3 says it outright: many citizen-science contributors may not be scientists. The mechanism is coverage and volume (a million backyards), not expertise.

misconceptions.heading: “Citizen science just collects opinions”
misconceptions.myth: Citizen-science projects are basically online polls — people click what they like, and scientists call the votes 'data.'
misconceptions.reality: EK IOC-1.E.3 requires contributors to supply relevant DATA to research using their own computing devices: photographs, measurements, sensor readings, observations. A popularity vote is crowdsourced input, but it is not research data — the distinction exam items exploit.

misconceptions.heading: “A big team at one company counts as crowdsourcing”
misconceptions.myth: Any large collaborative effort qualifies — fifty engineers at one firm are a 'crowd' working on a shared problem.
misconceptions.reality: The definition needs a large number of people contributing via the Internet — open participation at scale, not ordinary staffing. A hired team of any size is Topic 1.1 collaboration. The worked table's newspaper row (three meteorologists) drills the boundary, and quiz distractors reuse it.

support: Give the classification sentence frame: 'This project collects ______ from ______ people via ______, so it IS / IS NOT crowdsourcing, and its model is ______.' Students fill it for every scenario before writing free-form answers.

Anchor every scenario back to the bell ringer: 'Would this still work with 100 people? What is each stranger's 60-second task here?' If the crowd can shrink without killing the project, it is not a crowd project.
extension: Have deep-dive students design the quality-control layer for the bell-ringer project: how would the system catch bad or fake contributions without requiring expert contributors? Connect their rules to how real citizen-science platforms flag implausible reports.

Challenge students to find the crowdsourcing hiding in apps they already use — traffic rerouting, review scores, photo tagging — and identify what each app's crowd contributes and who profits from it (a preview of Topic 5.5's ownership questions).

discussion: A navigation app gets its live traffic data free from millions of drivers, then sells ads against the map those drivers built. Do the contributors deserve anything back — payment, control, credit — or is a faster commute payment enough?

Citizen-science projects accept data from anyone, then filter it with software and reviewers. Would you trust a medical study built partly on volunteer data from home devices? What would raise or lower your trust?

Crowds can fund a stranger's surgery in hours — and pile onto the wrong suspect in a breaking-news manhunt. What separates the crowds that enhance human capability from the crowds that misfire, and can a platform's design tell them apart in advance?
guide.answerKeyNote: Each question's full rationale is on the KEY copy of the quiz (print it with the answer-key option). The short answer key below is auto-derived from the live quiz block, so it can never drift from the student questions.
